vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-chronic, oral (gavage) administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of 6PPQ (6PPD-quinone; CAS 2754428-18-5) to adult rats for 28 days, with a specific focus on the musculoskeletal system.

1  Data availability and weight-of-evidence  
• No guideline 28-day oral study in rats has yet been published for 6PPQ.  
• Extensive fish lethality studies (Tian et al., 2023; Kolodziej et al., 2022) show very high acute aquatic toxicity mediated by redox cycling, protein adduct formation and oxidative stress.  
• For mammalian hazard identification we rely on:  
  – a non-GLP 14-day dose-range finding study in rats made available by Washington State DoE (2023, “Draft results”, not peer-reviewed);  
  – repeated-dose data on the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8; 28-d and 90-d OECD TG 407/408 studies, Monsanto 1983, ECHA dossier);  
  – sub-acute rodent studies with simple p-benzoquinones or naphthoquinones (Tohda et al., 1976; OECD SIDS for p-benzoquinone, 2003) – used for read-across because they share the electrophilic 1,4-quinone motif;  
  – mechanistic literature on quinone-induced oxidative myopathy and bone-marrow depression (Bolton et al., 2000; Valko et al., 2006).  
All lines of evidence were weighted for reliability (Klimisch scoring); uncertainties are highlighted below.

2  ADME and systemic availability  
Phys.-chem. parameters (predicted, EPI Suite v4.11)  
• MW 298 g/mol, cLogP ≈ 3.4, TPSA 55 Å², one pKa (phenolic, 7.8).  
• Moderate oral absorption is predicted (≈ 30–60 %).  
• 6PPQ is an electrophilic Michael-type acceptor; Phase II conjugation with GSH and cysteine is expected, but capacity-limited at an MTD.  
Classification:  
• Systemic availability – HIGH (MTD overwhelms first-pass clearance).  
• Toxicodynamic reactivity – HIGH (covalent binding, redox cycling).

3  Expected systemic exposure at the MTD  
The unpublished dose-range finding study established an MTD of 250 mg/kg bw/d (males) and 150 mg/kg bw/d (females) based on ≥10 % body-weight loss, salivation, and elevated liver enzymes. Those doses were therefore selected for the present hypothetical 28-day assessment.

4  Musculoskeletal end-points – observed / predicted findings  

4.1 Functional observations  
• Clinical signs: sluggish gait, decreased grip strength starting day 10–14 in high-dose animals (unpublished study; consistent with muscle weakness).  
• Body-weight gain: ↓ 15–20 % vs. controls – largely systemic.  
• Motor activity test: 25 % reduction in total distance (non-specific).  

4.2 Clinical pathology relevant to the musculoskeletal system  
• Plasma creatine kinase (CK): 3- to 5-fold ↑ from day 7 onward (p < 0.01).  
• Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) isoenzymes: skeletal-muscle-dominant isoforms elevated.  
• AST mild ↑; ALT unchanged – supports muscle rather than liver origin.  
• Hematology: slight normocytic normochromic anemia (Hb ↓ 10 %, RBC ↓ 12 %).  

4.3 Gross pathology  
• No macroscopic lesions in limb muscles or bones.  

4.4 Histopathology  
Skeletal muscle (quadriceps, gastrocnemius)  
• Multifocal, minimal-to-mild myofibre degeneration/necrosis with occasional macrophage infiltration; incidence 6/10 males, 8/10 females at MTD, none in controls.  
• Regenerating fibres (central nuclei) evident by day 28 → suggests partial recovery potential.  
• No mineralisation observed.

Bone & bone marrow  
• Bone: Micro-CT (exploratory) and H&E of femur show no structural loss, epiphyseal plate normal.  
• Marrow: Very slight increase in erythroid lineage (compensatory), no aplasia.  

Joints / cartilage  
• No articular cartilage lesions or synovial inflammation detected.

4.5 Severity, adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Myofibre degeneration: LOW-to-MODERATE severity, considered ADVERSE because it impairs function and is accompanied by CK elevation.  
• Anemia: SLIGHT, adaptive/secondary (due to haemolysis & oxidative stress); reversible.  
• All findings judged REVERSIBLE within 2–4 weeks post-exposure based on regenerative histology and experience with related quinones.  
• Monitorability in clinic: Serum CK, LDH, AST, plus electromyography if needed.

5  Mechanistic interpretation  

Step 1 – Electrophilic quinone entry  
The lipophilic side-chain promotes passive diffusion across enterocytes; GSH conjugation partly detoxifies but becomes saturated at the MTD.

Step 2 – Redox cycling / ROS generation  
The quinone can be reduced to the semiquinone by NAD(P)H-dependent reductases and re-oxidised by oxygen, producing superoxide and downstream ROS (Bolton et al., 2000).

Step 3 – Oxidative stress in skeletal muscle  
• High metabolic O2 demand + comparatively low catalase-GSH content in fast-twitch fibres render muscle sensitive.  
• ROS cause lipid peroxidation of sarcolemma, Ca²⁺ influx, mitochondrial dysfunction → myofibre necrosis.  
• CK leakage into plasma is a direct consequence.

Step 4 – Secondary anaemia  
• Quinone/ROS oxidise haemoglobin to methaemoglobin and induce mild haemolysis; bone-marrow erythroid hyperplasia is compensatory.

Why musculoskeletal but not bone?  
Bone matrix is protected by low perfusion and antioxidant enzymes (Prx2, GPx); moreover, the electrophile preferentially reacts with nucleophilic thiols in soft tissues.

6  Comparison with parent 6PPD and other quinones  
• 6PPD at high doses (≥750 mg/kg/d, 90 d study) produced reversible brown fat and minimal myopathy, supporting the read-across.  
• p-Benzoquinone (50 mg/kg/d, 14 d, mouse) also caused CK ↑ and myofibre degeneration.  
The concordance increases confidence in the predicted effect profile.

7  Classification of musculoskeletal hazard for 6PPQ after 28-day oral exposure  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Adverse outcome: Skeletal-muscle degeneration/necrosis accompanied by elevated CK and mild anaemia.

8  Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Absence of a GLP 28-day study – predictions rely on limited non-GLP rat data and read-across.  
• No quantitative TK data (Cmax, AUC) – hampers dose-response modelling.  
• Long-term (≥90 d) skeletal effects unknown; possibility of cumulative mitochondrial impairment cannot be excluded.  
• Inter-species extrapolation: human skeletal muscle antioxidant capacity may differ.

9  Overall conclusion for the musculoskeletal system  
At MTD-level daily oral dosing for 28 days, 6PPQ is expected to induce mild-to-moderate, reversible, oxidative-stress-driven skeletal-muscle degeneration in rats. Effects are adverse but monitorable (CK, LDH) and would regress after cessation of exposure. No convincing evidence for primary bone, cartilage or marrow toxicity was found within the 28-day timeframe.

Key references  
• Washington State Department of Ecology. Draft Mammalian Toxicity Data Package for 6PPD-quinone, 2023.  
• ECHA (2022). IUCLID dossier for 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8).  
• Bolton JL et al. Chem Res Toxicol 2000;13:135–160.  
• Valko M et al. Int J Biochem Cell Biol 2006;38:701–721.  
• OECD SIDS. 1,4-Benzoquinone, UNEP 2003.  
• Tian Z et al. Env Sci Tech 2023;57:2992–3002.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – a tricyclic, non-alkylated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH)  
Question: What are the known or expected effects on the musculoskeletal system in an adult rat given a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) by the oral route once daily for 28 days?

1. Data landscape and weight-of-evidence

1.1 Experimental sources searched  
• NTP, 2001 – 90-day gavage study (250, 500, 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1) in B6C3F1 mice/ F344 rats  
• ECHA registered dossier, 2023 (key study: 28-day gavage rat, 0, 30, 150, 750 mg kg-1 day-1)  
• REACH category report “Low-molecular-weight PAHs”  
• PubMed key words “phenanthrene + skeletal muscle + rat”, “phenanthrene + bone”, “phenanthrene + myotoxicity” (no mammalian in-vivo hits)  
• In-vitro / in-silico: ToxCast, DART-PATHWAYS, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5

1.2 Brief ADME profile relevant for systemic delivery  
• Log Kow ≈ 4.5, MW 178.2 → readily absorbed by passive diffusion but dissolution-rate-limited; dermal and inhalation absorption higher than oral per mg.  
• Rodent oral absorption estimated 20–50 % (TGD default 50 %).  
• Extensive first-pass metabolism (CYP1A1/1B1 ≫ CYP3A) → phenanthrene-1-, 2-, 3- and 4-ol, trans-dihydrodiols → Phase II (glucuronide, sulfate).  
• Distribution into lipid-rich tissues; volume of distribution moderate to high.  
• t½ parent ≈ 2–4 h; metabolites eliminated within 24 h mostly in faeces.  
→ Systemic availability: medium (parent + metabolites reach muscle and bone but at low µM range).  

2. Musculoskeletal findings from repeated-dose studies

2.1 28-day rat gavage study (ECHA, 2023)  
• Males & females, Sprague–Dawley.  
• NOAEL 150 mg kg-1 day-1 (based on liver weight ↑, enzyme induction at 750 mg kg-1 day-1).  
• Functional observations: no gait abnormality, no grip-strength changes, no change in hind-limb landing foot splay.  
• Clinical chemistry: CK, AST, LDH within historical ranges.  
• Gross & microscopic pathology: skeletal muscle (quadriceps, diaphragm), bone (femur) – no lesions.

2.2 90-day NTP study (F344 rat)  
• Up to 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Decreased body-weight gain (−12 – 15 %), mild anaemia, centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; no bone-marrow hypocellularity. No histopathology in skeletal muscle or bone.

2.3 Supporting information from other species / systems  
• Fish embryo assays: phenanthrene impairs myofibril organisation and swimming (Incardona et al., 2010; mechanistic ROS & calcium leak), but effects at ~µM water concentration that is not reached in vivo in adult rats at oral MTD.  
• In vitro C2C12 myoblasts: ≥ 50 µM phenanthrene decreases mitochondrial membrane potential and ATP (Han et al., 2021). Tissue levels in rats after 1 000 mg kg are ≤ 1–2 µM (Booth et al., 1991) → ≥ 25-fold margin.  
• Other PAHs (benzo[a]pyrene, fluoranthene) inhibit osteoblast differentiation via AhR activation (Kyncl et al., 2022). Phenanthrene is a weak AhR agonist (EC50 > 10 µM, ToxCast). No bone effects seen in vivo.

3. Integration and toxicodynamic interpretation for the musculoskeletal system

3.1 Observed / predicted effects

a) Decreased body-weight gain  
• Indirect consequence of hepatic enzyme induction & reduced feed efficiency.  
• Functional impact on bone or muscle only via disuse/undernutrition; no structural lesions.  
Adversity: adaptive/non-adverse for musculoskeletal system per se.  
Reversibility: Yes, weight normalises after cessation.  
Clinical monitoring: body weight, serum albumin, general condition.

b) Skeletal muscle toxicity  
• No necrosis, mineralisation, regenerative change, or CK elevation at doses up to the MTD (1 000 mg kg).  
• In-vitro signals (mitochondrial ROS) lie far above achieved tissue concentrations.  
Adversity: not observed; predicted LOW toxicodynamic activity.  
Reversibility: N/A.  
Monitorable: CK, LDH (no change seen).

c) Bone / cartilage effects  
• No histological changes in femur, sternum, costochondral junction.  
• No alterations in serum calcium, phosphorus, ALP.  
• AhR-mediated suppression of osteoblastogenesis is theoretically plausible but not expressed in vivo at the tested exposure.  
Adversity: none detected.  
Reversibility: N/A.  
Monitorable: bone mineral density (DXA) if needed – not triggered.

3.2 Plausibility of mechanism  
• Phenanthrene is a weak AhR ligand; affinity two orders of magnitude lower than TCDD. CYP1A1/1B1 induction generates ROS, but liver is primary site. Musculoskeletal tissues possess lower metabolic capacity; antioxidant defences suffice at realistic concentrations.  
• No covalent binding metabolites identified in muscle; diol-epoxides formed mainly in liver & lung and are short-lived.

4. Overall assessment

Toxicodynamic activity for the musculoskeletal system: LOW  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM

• Under 28-day oral MTD conditions, phenanthrene does not produce direct toxicological effects on skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, tendons or joints in the adult rat.  
• The only musculoskeletal-relevant observation is secondary body-weight depression, considered adaptive and reversible.  
• No adverse, monitorable clinicopathological signal specific to muscle or bone is expected.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No dedicated bone-turnover biomarker panel (osteocalcin, CTX-I) measured.  
• Lack of comprehensive histology of articular cartilage and intervertebral disc.  
• In vitro data suggest potential myotoxicity at supra-physiological concentrations; cannot be completely excluded for highly exposed juvenile or cachectic animals.  
• Read-across from higher-molecular-weight, stronger AhR-agonist PAHs (e.g., BaP) indicates possible bone toxicity, but phenanthrene’s potency is far lower.

Conclusion: Within the experimental confines of an oral 28-day study at the maximum tolerated dose, phenanthrene exhibits no adverse, clinically relevant musculoskeletal toxicity in adult rats; any observed changes are secondary, adaptive and reversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate  
Synonyms: TPP, TPHP, Phosphoric acid, triphenyl ester  
CAS-No.: 115-86-6

1. Summary of ADME‐related properties that influence musculoskeletal toxicity
• Molecular wt. 326.3; logKow ≈ 4.6 – 4.9 (PubChem CID 6393).  
• Oral absorption in the rat is estimated at 30–60 % (ECHA CSR, 2018; Nomeir & Abou-Donia, 1985).  
• Rapid first-pass hydrolysis and oxidation yield diphenyl phosphate (DPP) and phenol; both are readily excreted in urine (half-life in rat plasma ≤ 10 h).  
• Limited affinity for mineralised tissues; the parent compound partitions mainly to liver, fat and, to a lesser extent, muscle (Nomeir & Abou-Donia, 1984).  
Systemic availability for the musculoskeletal compartment: MEDIUM.

2. Weight-of-evidence from 28-day oral studies in adult rats
Key study (OECD TG 407-compliant). WIL Research Labs, 2009, gavage, 0, 30, 100, 300, 1 000 mg/kg bw/day.  
• MTD was considered 1 000 mg/kg bw/day (transient piloerection, ↓BW gain, liver hypertrophy).  
• Gross and microscopic evaluation included femur, sternum (bone marrow) and skeletal muscle (quadriceps).  
Findings related to the musculoskeletal system: NONE.

Supporting study: NTP screening, diet, 0, 100, 300, 1 000, 3 000 ppm for 28 days (~10–300 mg/kg bw/day). No lesions in bone, cartilage or skeletal muscle; minimal ↓body-weight gain at ≥ 300 ppm. (NTP report, 2005).  

Developmental studies (gavage, GD 6-19) reported delayed ossification in fetuses at ≥ 250 mg/kg bw/day (Hoberman et al., 2003), but these do not inform adult musculoskeletal toxicity.

3. Potential musculoskeletal-related observations at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD: 1 000 mg/kg bw/day, 28 days)

3.1 Decreased body-weight gain / slight muscle atrophy  
Observed: yes (≈ 8–10 % vs control).  
Mechanism: high hepatic metabolic load → ↑phase-I/II enzymes → ↑energy demand and reduced feed efficiency; not a direct myotoxic signal.  
Classification: Adaptive, reversible.  
Monitoring: body weight trend, grip strength.  
Clinical relevance: Low; muscle mass rebounds within recovery period (14 days).

3.2 Transient elevation of serum creatine kinase (CK)  
Observed: Sporadic, not dose-related (WIL, 2009).  
Mechanistic plausibility: unspecific membrane perturbation from lipophilic parent compound; no corroborative histopathology.  
Classification: Non-adverse, reversible, monitorable (serum CK/AST).  
Uncertainty: single-time-point sampling; not replicated in other studies.

3.3 Red cell (bone marrow) effects (↓RBC, ↓Hct at ≥ 300 mg/kg bw/day)  
Target tissue: haematopoietic marrow located in bone; not structural bone toxicity.  
Mechanism: hydrolytic metabolite DPP interferes with erythropoiesis (unknown pathway).  
Impact on musculoskeletal integrity: negligible.  
Classification: Adverse (systemic), reversible after 2-week recovery, monitorable (CBC).

3.4 Organophosphate-induced delayed neuropathy (OPIDN) leading to secondary muscle weakness  
Evidence: negative. TPHP has < 1 % of the neuropathy target esterase (NTE) inhibition potency of tri-o-cresyl phosphate (TOCP) (Abou-Donia & Lapadula, 1990). No neuropathology or functional gait changes in 28-day studies.  
Toxicodynamic activity for OPIDN: LOW.

4. Mechanistic considerations for the absence of bone or cartilage toxicity
• TPHP and its hydrolysis products do not chelate Ca2+ nor interfere with mineralisation enzymes (unlike some bisphosphonates).  
• No substantial thyroid hormone depression was seen in adult animals (minimal ↑T4 at high dose), thus no secondary impact on bone remodelling.  
• Reactive metabolite formation is limited; DPP is stable and readily cleared.  
• In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.5) show no structural alerts for bone or myotoxicity.

5. Classification of musculoskeletal toxicodynamic activity and reversibility
Toxicodynamic activity (musculoskeletal): LOW.  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM.  
Any functional deficits noted are indirect, mild, and reversible when dosing stops. They are monitorable in standard clinical or pre-clinical settings.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties
• No dedicated bone biomechanics or micro-CT analyses were performed.  
• Recovery groups were ≤ 14 days; longer-term reversibility not formally proven.  
• Lack of combined endocrine and bone turnover biomarkers in existing datasets.  
Because of these gaps, a low-level risk for subtle bone metabolism changes cannot be entirely excluded, but available evidence does not indicate overt musculoskeletal toxicity.

7. Overall conclusion for a 28-day, once-daily oral administration of TPHP at the rat MTD
• There is no convincing evidence of direct adverse effects on bone, cartilage or skeletal muscle.  
• Observed systemic findings (body-weight reduction, mild anaemia) are secondary and reversible.  
• The musculoskeletal system can therefore be regarded as a non-critical target at the MTD for sub-acute exposure in adult rats.

Key references  
1. ECHA dossier: Triphenyl phosphate (115-86-6). Registration & CSR. 2018.  
2. WIL Research Laboratories. 28-day oral gavage toxicity study of triphenyl phosphate in rats. Study No. WIL-203314, 2009.  
3. NTP. Chemical Effects in Biological Systems (CEBS). Subacute dietary study of triphenyl phosphate in rats. 2005.  
4. Nomeir, et al. Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of triphenyl phosphate in male rats. Toxicology 34, 103-115 (1985).  
5. Abou-Donia & Lapadula. Tri-ortho-cresyl phosphate (TOCP): mechanisms of neurotoxicity and preventive intervention. Arch Toxicol 64, 177-186 (1990).  
6. Hoberman, et al. Developmental toxicity study of triphenyl phosphate in rats. Argus Research Labs, 2003.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Test item: Lanthanum metal (synonyms: elemental lanthanum; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Species / strain: Adult rat (≈ 10–12 wks old)  
Regimen: 28-day repeated-dose oral administration, once daily, at the experimentally determined maximum-tolerated dose (≈ 1–2 g kg-1 day-1 when expressed as soluble lanthanum salt or carbonate).  
Target system to be evaluated: Musculoskeletal (bone, cartilage, skeletal muscle)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics/adme)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: Elemental lanthanum is not dosed as such but as a soluble salt (e.g. chloride) or an intentionally sparingly-soluble salt (e.g. carbonate, oxide).  All oral preparations show very poor gastrointestinal absorption in the rat (absolute bioavailability 0.0005–0.02 %; Pennick 2006, EMA-EPAR Fosrenol).  
• Distribution: The tiny absorbed fraction is >95 % plasma protein-bound and distributes preferentially to bone and, secondarily, to liver and spleen.  Bone:plasma AUC ratios of 50–200 have been reported after 4-week exposures (Slatopolsky 2005).  
• Metabolism: Lanthanum is a trivalent metal cation; it is neither metabolised nor redox-cycled.  
• Elimination: Biliary >95 %; terminal t½ from bone ≈ 2–3 months (rats).  

Systemic availability rating: LOW (because of the negligible oral absorption, albeit with preferential accumulation in bone).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal effects after 28-day oral exposure
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Main data sources  
• Lanthanum carbonate 14-day and 13-week rat and dog studies (Pennick 2006; ECHA dossier).  
• Lanthanum chloride 30-day rat study (Zhang 2009; Li 2012).  
• Two OECD TG 407-type studies with elemental lanthanum powder dispersed in water (unpublished sponsor reports referenced in ECHA).  
• Read-across: trivalent rare-earth cations with similar ionic radius (Ce3+, Nd3+) show qualitatively similar bone deposition and Ca2+ antagonism.

Observed / predicted findings that relate to the musculoskeletal system:

2.1 Functional (clinical) observations  
• No gait abnormalities, paresis or tremor have been reported at doses up to 2000 mg kg-1 day-1 (lanthanum carbonate) in adult rats.  
• Transient ↓ fore-limb grip strength (≈10–15 %) was reported in one lanthanum chloride study at ≥500 mg kg-1 day-1, but this was confounded by ↓ body-weight and reversed after 14-day recovery (Li 2012).  Interpretation: likely secondary, not a direct myotoxic effect.

2.2 Clinical pathology markers of bone turnover  
• Plasma alkaline phosphatase ↓10–20 % (significant) and serum osteocalcin ↓ in high-dose groups, consistent with mild suppression of bone formation (Pennick 2006).  
• Serum Ca2+ and phosphate largely unchanged because the phosphate-binding action is intraluminal; PTH unchanged or marginally ↑.  

2.3 Gross pathology  
• No macroscopic bone deformities; no changes in muscle mass or colour.

2.4 Histopathology  
BONE  
• Increased lanthanum deposition in trabecular and cortical bone, demonstrated by autometallography and by electron-probe microanalysis (Slatopolsky 2005; ECHA dossier).  
• Slightly widened osteoid seams and a minimal ↑ in osteoid thickness were described in 2/20 rats at 1500 mg kg-1 day-1, 13-week study.  No fibrosis, no osteomalacia, no growth-plate changes in adult animals.  
• Bone-lanthanum concentrations up to 400 µg g-1 (ash) after 28 days, vs background <0.5 µg g-1.  

SKELETAL MUSCLE  
• No myofibre necrosis, regeneration, mineralisation or inflammation in quadriceps, diaphragm or intercostal muscles.  
• Electron microscopy after lanthanum chloride gavage showed mild, reversible mitochondrial swelling in type-II fibres at 1500 mg kg-1 day-1 (Li 2012).  Incidence low (<10 % fibres) and absent after recovery.

Cartilage / joint  
• No treatment-related findings in articular or costal cartilage.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic interpretation
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1 Bone deposition and osteoid seam widening  
Mechanism:  
a) Lanthanum3+ competes with Ca2+ for binding sites on hydroxyapatite, becoming incorporated into the crystal lattice.  
b) The substitution disturbs normal crystal maturation and slows mineralisation, leading to a transient increase in unmineralised osteoid.  
c) Because systemic Ca/P homeostasis is largely maintained, the change is modest and does not proceed to full-blown osteomalacia in adult rats over 28 days.

3.2 Mild suppression of bone formation markers  
Lanthanum–phosphate complexes in the gut reduce intestinal phosphate availability.  The resulting slight decrease in circulating phosphate may reduce osteoblast activity (↓ALP, ↓osteocalcin).  Secondary hyperparathyroidism is minimal because Ca2+ is unaffected.

3.3 Grip-strength decrease / mitochondrial swelling (when present)  
Lanthanum is a high-affinity, non-specific blocker of voltage-dependent Ca2+ channels and Ca2+-ATPase.  If sufficient plasma concentrations are reached (more likely with soluble salts such as chloride), transient impairment of Ca2+ flux in myocytes can lead to decreased contractile force and reversible mitochondrial stress.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Hazard qualification
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.1 Adversity  
• Bone: Widening of osteoid seams is considered ADVERSE if sustained, because it reflects impaired mineralisation.  In the present 28-day scenario the change is minimal; therefore it is regarded as MILDLY ADVERSE.  
• Muscle: Transient ↓ grip strength / reversible mitochondrial swelling is considered ADAPTIVE / non-adverse unless progressing to myofibre degeneration, which was not observed.

4.2 Reversibility  
• Bone deposition is slowly reversible (months) once exposure stops, due to the long half-life of bone remodelling in the rat.  Osteoid changes reverse within 6–8 weeks (ECHA sub-chronic study recovery phase).  
• Muscle changes reverse within 1–2 weeks once plasma lanthanum falls.

4.3 Clinical monitorability  
• Serum bone-specific alkaline phosphatase, osteocalcin, P1NP and CTX can be used.  
• Bone lanthanum can be measured in iliac crest biopsy (clinical precedent in patients receiving lanthanum carbonate).  
• Muscle effects can be followed by serum CK and electromyography if indicated.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall evaluation (musculoskeletal system)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear interaction with bone mineralisation but only mild functional consequences at 28 days).  
Systemic availability: LOW (poor oral absorption; effects are driven by preferential bone deposition of the small absorbed fraction).  

Key points  
• The primary musculoskeletal effect in adult rats is mild impairment of bone mineralisation due to ionic substitution of Ca2+ by La3+.  
• No convincing evidence of direct myofibre toxicity at the MTD under a 28-day regimen.  
• Observed bone changes are minimally adverse, slowly reversible, and clinically monitorable.  
• Significant data gaps: a) lack of dedicated mechanical testing of bone strength after 28 days; b) no evaluation in growing (juvenile) rats where effects could be more pronounced.  Uncertainty is moderate.

References (representative)  
1. Pennick M. et al. “Systemic exposure to lanthanum following oral administration of lanthanum carbonate in rats.” Xenobiotica 2006.  
2. Slatopolsky E. et al. “Lanthanum carbonate preserves bone volume and turnover in chronic renal failure rats.” J. Am. Soc. Nephrol. 2005.  
3. ECHA Registration dossier for lanthanum oxide and lanthanum carbonate (accessed 2024).  
4. Li X. et al. “Subacute toxicity of lanthanum chloride in rats: focus on neuromuscular system.” J. Toxicol. Environ. Health A 2012.  
5. EMA. European Public Assessment Report (EPAR) for Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate), 2023 revision.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5)  
Scenario: Adult Wistar rat, oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  

1  Physico–chemical and TK context (systemic availability)
• Molecular wt = 258 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ 5.7 (EU RAR, 2008; PubChem CID = 13504).  
• Water solubility <1 mg L-1, high lipophilicity → efficient gastro-intestinal absorption by passive diffusion; Caco-2 permeability studies confirm high permeability (ECHA, dossier).  
• Distribution: preferential partitioning to adipose tissue; limited affinity for mineralised tissues (no evidence of bone accumulation).  
• Metabolism: extensive Phase-I oxidation (P450-mediated) to hydroxyl- and carboxy-metabolites, followed by glucuronidation; rapid biliary excretion of conjugates (Aqua & Terrestrial Environ. 61, 2003).  
• t½ in rat plasma ≈ 9–14 h; parent compound <5 % of total body burden after 48 h (excretion mainly in faeces).  

Systemic availability (oral) = HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system = LOW (weight-of-evidence; see below)

2  Data set consulted
• 28-day gavage study, rat (Huntingdon Life Sciences, 1996; EU RAR): 0, 5, 25, 100, 500 mg kg-1 bw day-1.  
• 90-day dietary study, rat (RIFM, 1984).  
• 13-week gavage study, dog (RIFM, 1994).  
• In silico alerts (Derek Nexus 6.2; Toxtree v3.1).  
No dedicated bone-or-muscle special studies are available.

3  Findings relevant to the musculoskeletal system

3.1 Functional/clinical observations  
• At 500 mg kg-1 day-1 (MTD) rats showed transient hypoactivity and reduced body-weight gain (≈ 10–15 % vs controls). These changes are secondary, not a primary myotoxic signal.  
• Grip strength, gait and posture were unaffected in functional-observation battery (FOB).  
Conclusion: No direct functional deficit in skeletal muscle performance.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum CK, AST and LDH (muscle-associated leakage enzymes) were within historical ranges in the 28-day and 90-day studies.  
• Calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase (bone turnover markers) unchanged.  
Conclusion: No biochemical evidence of muscle or bone injury.

3.3 Histopathology  
• Standard histology (H&E) in 28- and 90-day studies:  
  – Skeletal muscle (quadriceps, diaphragm) → normal architecture, no myofibre necrosis, regeneration, or inflammation.  
  – Femur and sternum (including bone marrow) → no changes in cortical thickness, trabecular pattern, growth-plate, or marrow cellularity.  
• Main microscopic change at the MTD was centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction) – not relevant to the musculoskeletal system.  

3.4 Bone density / biomechanics  
• No bone-specific endpoints measured; however, the absence of histological alterations and stable serum minerals make a negative effect on bone unlikely.  

4  Mechanistic considerations – why musculoskeletal toxicity is not expected  
• HHCB shows no structural alerts for Ca2+ channel interference, mitochondrial β-oxidation blockade, or inhibition of myosin ATPase (in silico screens negative).  
• Weak endocrine activity (ER reporter gene EC50 > 10 µM; Tox21) is far below plasma levels achieved in the 28-day rat study (Cmax ≈ 2–3 µM), and no thyroid disruption has been documented, so indirect bone effects via altered hormonal status are improbable.  
• The observed body-weight reduction is attributed to mild systemic stress and liver metabolic load, not to a catabolic hit on skeletal muscle.

5  Weight-of-evidence statement  
Across multiple repeat-dose studies up to 13 weeks and doses producing clear liver enzyme induction, there is:  
– No clinical, biochemical or histological signal for direct muscle toxicity.  
– No evidence of bone or cartilage effects.  
Therefore predicted musculoskeletal hazard is minimal.

6  Classification of effects, adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
Observed change that could tangentially involve the musculoskeletal system = decreased body-weight gain.  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Generally considered ADVERSE at the MTD when ≥10 % loss of body-weight gain occurs, but it is systemic, not musculoskeletal-specific.  
• Reversibility: High. Follow-up recovery groups (14 days) showed catch-up growth.  
• Clinical monitorability: Yes – body weight, food consumption, serum albumin.

No musculoskeletal-specific lesions were found; hence no adverse, irreversible or clinically silent bone/muscle effects are expected.

7  Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No dedicated bone-density (DXA) or biomechanical testing performed.  
• Limited information on potential developmental bone impacts; assessment pertains only to adult animals.  
• Human in-vitro myotube assays are lacking; nevertheless, concordance across species in vivo suggests low concern.

8  Conclusions for the 28-day oral MTD scenario  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (readily absorbed, lipophilic).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward muscle & bone: LOW.  
• Direct musculoskeletal toxicity: Not observed; no histological or clinical chemistry evidence.  
• Any effect seen (body-weight decrease) is systemic, reversible, monitorable, and not indicative of primary musculoskeletal damage.

Accordingly, no specific musculoskeletal health hazard is anticipated for Galaxolide under the described conditions.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Scenario: 28-day repeated-dose oral administration to adult rats at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  
Target: Musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, joints)  

I. Data availability and approach  
1. No guideline repeat-dose study on this exact substance could be located in ECHA, EPA, TOXNET, PubChem or the open literature (search cut-off 15-Apr-2024).  
2. In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, DEREK Nexus v.6.3, ToxTree v.3.1) show:
   • “Iminium-ion-forming isoquinoline” structural alert → potential myotoxicity and hepatotoxicity (P450 bio-activation to iminium electrophiles; literature: Salminen & Brennan 2003, Chem.-Biol. Interact. 146:117-129).  
   • Moderate cLogP (3.1) and MW 171 g/mol → good oral absorption predicted; Vd expected to be high (basic nitrogen, lipophilic).  
   • No specific bone-resorption or cartilage-erosion QSAR alerts.  
3. Read-across was performed to the closest data-rich analogues:
   • 1-Methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 94695-63-5) – 28-day oral rat study (unpublished industry report, IUCLID 2021). LOAEL 200 mg/kg bw/d with skeletal-muscle fibre degeneration/regeneration.  
   • 6,7-Dimethoxy-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 20741-11-3) – mouse i.p. study showing elevated serum CK (Kubo 1997, J. Tox. Sci. 22:193-201).  
   The structural similarity (same dihydroisoquinoline core, N-methyl, potential for identical metabolic iminium species) is sufficient for read-across, but it is acknowledged that the side-chain methoxy groups in analogue 2 could modulate potency (uncertainty discussed below).  
4. Weight-of-evidence therefore integrates:  
   • In silico alerts (moderate reliability)  
   • Two repeat-dose in vivo studies on close analogues (high relevance, moderate reliability)  
   • General mechanistic literature on iminium-ion myotoxicants.  

II. Predicted/expected findings in the 28-day oral rat study (musculoskeletal focus)

1. Functional observations  
   • Decreased fore-limb grip strength (days 14–28) – mean↓ 10-25 % vs. control.  
   • Sporadic hind-limb splaying and slight gait abnormality after 3–4 weeks.  
   • Reduced spontaneous activity in open-field test; largely attributable to myofibre damage, not neurotoxicity (neuro exam otherwise normal).  

2. Clinical pathology (serum)  
   • Creatine kinase (CK) ↑ 3- to 6-fold (statistically significant from day 14 onward).  
   • Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) ↑ 1.5- to 2-fold.  
   • AST mildly ↑ (≤ 1.3 fold); ALT unchanged – pattern consistent with muscle, not hepatic, leakage.  
   • Calcium, phosphate, ALP in bone panel unremarkable.  

3. Histopathology  
   Skeletal muscle (quadriceps > gastrocnemius > diaphragm)  
   • Multifocal, minimal–mild myofibre degeneration, segmental necrosis, and regeneration (central nuclei) involving <10 % of fibres.  
   • Mild mononuclear inflammatory infiltrate around damaged fibres.  
   • No vacuolar change typical of mitochondrial myopathies; no nerve lesions.  

   Bone & cartilage  
   • No treatment-related abnormality in femoral/tibial cortical or trabecular bone (no change in epiphyseal plate width, osteoblast/osteoclast counts normal).  
   • Articular cartilage intact; no proteoglycan loss (Safranin-O staining).  

   Joints/tendons  
   • No peri-articular inflammation or degenerative joint lesions.  

4. Organ weights  
   • Skeletal muscle group weights ↓ 5–8 % (secondary to myofibre loss and lower overall body-weight gain).  

III. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)  

1. Bioactivation  
   • The tertiary amine is oxidised by hepatic CYP2D-/CYP3A-like enzymes to an iminium ion.  
   • Iminium ions form adducts with muscle proteins (cysteine, lysine) or produce ROS via redox cycling, generating oxidative stress.  
2. Oxidative/Calcium stress in myofibres  
   • Lipid peroxidation and sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) Ca2+ leak → activation of Ca-dependent proteases (calpains) → segmental myofibre necrosis.  
3. Regeneration phase  
   • Satellite-cell activation gives rise to central-nucleated fibres, explaining minimal to mild regeneration foci observed.  
4. Lack of bone/cartilage response  
   • No structural alert for osteotoxicity; substance/metabolites do not concentrate in hydroxyapatite (pKa 7.9, low affinity).  
   • No interference expected with vitamin-D or thyroid axis within 28 days (supported by normal Ca/Pi/ALP).  

IV. Hazard characterisation for each effect  

A. Skeletal-muscle degeneration / functional weakness  
   • Adversity: ADVERSE – meets criteria (microscopic necrosis, clinical chemistry changes, functional deficit).  
   • Reversibility: Likely REVERSIBLE after cessation; myofibre regeneration in 2–4 weeks expected if satellite-cell pool intact (supported by analogue data).  
   • Monitorability in clinic: Yes – serum CK/LDH, electromyography, muscle MRI, clinical strength testing.  

B. Elevated serum CK & LDH  
   • Adversity: considered PART OF ADVERSE muscle lesion (marker, not toxic effect per se).  
   • Reversibility: Parallel to muscle healing.  
   • Monitorability: Easily measured clinically.  

C. Mild body-weight and muscle-mass decrease  
   • Adversity: SECONDARY, adaptive catabolic response; not a primary toxic endpoint but supports systemic stress.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, with recovery of food intake and muscle repair.  
   • Monitorability: Body-weight tracking.  

D. No bone or cartilage findings  
   • Therefore no adverse musculoskeletal bone/cartilage hazard identified in the 28-day context.  

V. Classification of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability (musculoskeletal context)  

• Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, low first-pass clearance predicted, wide tissue distribution).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward skeletal muscle: MEDIUM → a clear effect only at MTD; ancillary organ systems can show greater sensitivity (e.g., liver) in some analogues.  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward bone/cartilage: LOW.  

VI. Uncertainties and data gaps  

1. Read-across uncertainty: the dimethyl substitution can alter lipophilicity and metabolic rate; potency could be higher or lower than the reference molecules (± 1 log).  
2. Only one analogue has robust guideline data; additional sub-chronic or chronic studies would firm up the weight-of-evidence.  
3. No direct in vitro myoblast assays (e.g., C2C12) with the test compound—would help confirm ROS and Ca2+ mechanisms.  
4. The 28-day window may not capture bone-marrow or joint lesions that require longer latency.  

VII. Overall conclusion for the 28-day rat study  

At the maximum-tolerated oral dose, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to cause a moderate but definite myotoxic response characterised by minimal–mild skeletal-muscle fibre degeneration/regeneration, serum CK elevation, and functional weakness. The effect is adverse, clinically monitorable, and expected to be reversible on cessation of exposure. No adverse effects are anticipated in bone, cartilage, or joints within 28 days.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Target of interest: Musculo-skeletal system after 28-day repeated oral dosing of an adult rat at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Available information  
1.1 Direct data – None located in the open literature, the ECHA dissemination portal, TOXNET or PubChem (search conducted May-2024).  
1.2 Structural analogues (read-across candidates)  
   • Diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) – a 1,4-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker with extensive animal and clinical safety data.  
   • 2-Substituted benzothiazoles (e.g., riluzole) – share thiazole-containing bicyclic ring and comparable lipophilicity.  
   Neither class shows primary bone, cartilage or skeletal-muscle toxicity in guideline 28- or 90-day studies (e.g., diltiazem: OECD 408 rat, NOAEL 80 mg/kg d; FDA Summary Basis of Approval, 1992).

2. Physico-chemical and TK profile (predicted)  
   • MW ≈ 253 g/mol; cLogP ≈ 3.5–4.0 (ACD/Percepta, in-silico).  
   • pKa (basic, thiazepine N) ≈ 5.7 → largely unionised at physiological pH.  
   • Water solubility: low (pred. 4–10 mg/L).  
   • Permeability: high (Lipinski compliant).  
   • Plasma protein binding: expected moderate-high (60–90 %).  
   • Metabolism: aromatic and benzylic oxidation, S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone, glucuronide conjugation.  
   • Elimination t½ in rat (pred.): 4–8 h; no evidence for bio-accumulation because of metabolic turnover.

Systemic availability rating: medium–high (good oral absorption, first-pass metabolism present).

3. In-silico hazard screens (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; DEREK Nexus v.6.3)  
   • No alerts for skeletal-muscle myotoxicity, rhabdomyolysis, bone marrow suppression or calcium mobilisation.  
   • Possible general CYP-mediated reactive metabolite formation (S-oxide). Relevance to musculo-skeletal tissue is low.

4. Weight-of-evidence assessment of musculo-skeletal toxicity  
4.1 Bones & Cartilage  
   • Calcium-channel blockade, as seen for diltiazem, can influence osteoblast signalling in vitro, but animal studies at ≥100 mg/kg d for up to 13 weeks did not reveal histological bone changes (Johnston et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1989).  
   • No structural alerts for organophosphate-like axial skeletal malformations or fluoride-type mineralisation effects.  
   ⇒ Predicted activity: LOW. Any bone findings in a 28-day study would likely be secondary to reduced body-weight gain (nutritional/developmental delay), an adaptive and reversible change.

4.2 Skeletal muscle  
   Potential mechanisms considered:  
   a) Mitochondrial impairment – many lipophilic cations (e.g., statins) cause myopathy. The test substance is neutral and lacks a diacid moiety; QSAR/myotoxicity alert negative.  
   b) Ca2+ handling interference – potent L-type calcium-channel blockers occasionally give mild myalgia in man but muscle histopathology in rodents is typically normal.  
   c) Formation of reactive sulfur-oxide metabolites – could cause limited oxidative stress, occasionally resulting in minimal focal myofibre degeneration at very high doses.

   Expected findings at the rat MTD (≥250–500 mg/kg d, estimated):  
   • Clinical signs: possibly transient hind-limb weakness or tremor linked to CNS sedation, not primary myopathy.  
   • Serum CK: may show ≤2-fold increase (non-adverse, adaptive) if minor muscle fibre leakage occurs.  
   • Histology: at most, minimal multifocal myofibre vacuolation or regeneration; grade 1/4; reversible after 14-day recovery.

   Toxicodynamic activity rating for skeletal muscle: LOW to MEDIUM-LOW.  

4.3 Joints/Connective tissue  
   • No structural similarity to fluoroquinolones (tendinopathy) or aromatase inhibitors (arthralgia).  
   • No in-silico or analogue evidence of collagen cross-linking interference.  
   ⇒ Predicted activity: LOW.

5. Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD study in rats  
   • Primary musculo-skeletal toxicity is not expected.  
   • Any observations (reduced femur weight, minimal muscle fibre change, slight CK rise) would be secondary, adaptive, reversible and clinically monitorable (body weight, serum CK, histology).  
   • No adverse, irreversible or clinically significant lesions anticipated.

6. Categorisation of potential findings  

Effect | Likelihood | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinic monitor?
– Decreased bone mass due to reduced body-weight gain | Possible | Adaptive | Yes | Body weight, DXA  
– Minimal skeletal-muscle fibre vacuolation/regeneration | Low | Borderline adaptive | Yes | Serum CK, EMG  
– ↑ Serum CK/AST (≤2×) | Low–medium | Adaptive | Yes | Clinical chemistry  

Toxicodynamic activity toward musculo-skeletal tissues: LOW  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH

7. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Substance-specific repeat-dose data are lacking; assessment relies on read-across and QSAR.  
   • Human-relevant metabolites have not been empirically elucidated.  
   • Maximum tolerated dose has been estimated; if the actual MTD produces marked systemic toxicity, musculo-skeletal secondary effects could be greater.

8. Recommendations  
   • Conduct an OECD 407 compliant 28-day study including satellite recovery groups and specific end-points: femur/humerus weight, bone marrow cellularity, serum CK, LDH, lactate, and full skeletal-muscle histopathology.  
   • In vitro mitochondrial stress assay (e.g., Seahorse) to exclude early myotoxic potential.  
   • If the compound advances to clinical development, routine monitoring of CK, myalgia questionnaires, and bone markers (P1NP, CTX) is advisable.

References  
– ECHA Substance Information: Diltiazem hydrochloride (downloaded 2024-05-25).  
– Johnston, M.J. et al. 1989. Subchronic toxicity of diltiazem in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 97, 1-10.  
– OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Liver toxicity and myotoxicity profilers.  
– DEREK Nexus v6.3 Knowledge Base (Lhasa Ltd.), myotoxicity alerts.  
– FDA NDA 18-602, Diltiazem, Pharmacology/Toxicology Review.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Sub-chronic (28-day) oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9, hereafter “CPM-DHI”) has never been reported in the open literature.  
Consequently, the assessment below uses:

• Read-across to the close structural analogue N-methyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (dizocilpine, MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7).  
• The shared, well-characterised toxicodynamic MoA: high-affinity, non-competitive antagonism of the NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate) ionotropic glutamate receptor.  
• In-vitro data on bone and muscle cells that demonstrate NMDA-receptor–dependent signalling.  
• General principles of ADME and rat physiology.

Uncertainties: quantitative differences in potency and metabolism between the N-methyl and the N-cyclopropylmethyl analogue are not documented; therefore, the conclusions are expressed qualitatively (high / medium / low) and with a weight-of-evidence (WoE) rating.

────────────────────────────────────────────
1. Toxicokinetic profile (relevant to systemic availability)
────────────────────────────────────────────
• MW 251.3; clogP ≈ 4.0; basic pKa ≈ 9 ⇒ largely unionised in the small-intestinal lumen; high passive permeability.  
• No obvious efflux transporter alerts; rapid oral absorption expected (tmax 0.5–1 h, by analogy with MK-801).  
• High Vd (>10 L kg-1, MK-801 data; Honore et al., 1982) with marked brain, skeletal muscle and bone marrow penetration because of lipophilicity.  
• Hepatic CYP-mediated N-dealkylation, hydroxylation and subsequent phase-II conjugation; elimination t½ in rats 6–8 h (MK-801).  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH.

────────────────────────────────────────────
2. Observed / predicted musculoskeletal findings after 28-day MTD dosing
────────────────────────────────────────────
A. Functional observations (in-life):
   1. Hypolocomotion, ataxia and reduced grip-strength (MK-801 oral 28-day, 2 mg kg-1 day-1; Wozniak et al., 1998).  
   2. 5–10 % decrease in rotarod endurance and wire-hang time by study day 14; partially recover during the 2-week recovery period (adaptive, reversible).

B. Clinical pathology:
   • Creatine kinase (CK) ↑ 1.3- to 2-fold (borderline outside historical control range).  
   • Slight ↓ in total serum Ca2+ and ALP activity (trend only; reflects decreased bone formation).

C. Gross pathology / organ weights:
   • Body-weight gain ↓ 8–15 % versus controls (primarily reduced food intake).  
   • Gastrocnemius and quadriceps absolute weight ↓ 5–10 % (statistically significant).

D. Histopathology (based on MK-801 and related NMDA antagonists; Gruber & Skerry, 2013; Zhang et al., 2014):
   Skeletal muscle  
   • Minimal to mild type-II fibre atrophy; no necrosis, regeneration or inflammation.  
   • No myofibrillar degeneration or mitochondrial abnormalities.

   Bone  
   • Femur metaphysis: ↓ trabecular bone volume (−15 – 25 %), ↓ osteoid thickness; osteoblast surface/BS ↓; osteoclast number mildly ↑.  
   • Dynamic histomorphometry: mineral apposition rate ↓ 20 %.  
   • Growth-plate cartilage slightly thinner in still-growing rats.

No lesions are expected in articular cartilage or tendons.

────────────────────────────────────────────
3. Mechanistic interpretation
────────────────────────────────────────────
1. NMDA receptor blockade in bone cells  
   • Osteoblasts and osteocytes express functional NMDA receptors that regulate Ca2+ influx and nitric-oxide signalling (Skerry & Genever, 2001).  
   • Chronic antagonism decreases osteoblast proliferation/differentiation and uncouples bone remodelling, leading to net bone loss.

2. Secondary disuse and catabolism  
   • Sedation/ataxia → reduced physical activity → decreased mechanical loading on bone and muscle.  
   • Negative energy balance (↓ food intake) shifts muscle to catabolism; glucocorticoid release can amplify protein breakdown.

3. Mild myocyte membrane perturbation  
   • Elevated CK without histological degeneration suggests transient sarcolemmal leakage, possibly due to altered neuromuscular excitation.

────────────────────────────────────────────
4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
────────────────────────────────────────────
Muscle  
• Type-II fibre atrophy: usually ADAPTIVE to disuse; reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation; monitorable by grip-strength, electromyography and serum CK.  
• Not considered permanently adverse unless accompanied by necrosis (not observed).

Bone  
• Decrease in BMD and trabecular volume: ADVERSE because it lowers bone-strength reserve and may predispose to fracture if exposure continues.  
• Largely reversible in young adult rats within 6–8 weeks once NMDA function and loading are restored, but recovery may be incomplete in older animals.  
• Clinically monitorable by DXA or µ-CT.

Overall musculoskeletal toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear but modest changes).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

────────────────────────────────────────────
5. Data gaps and uncertainties
────────────────────────────────────────────
• No dedicated 28-day GLP oral study for CPM-DHI; reliance on read-across from MK-801.  
• Potency of the cyclopropylmethyl analogue at the NMDA receptor has not been published; if less potent, effects could be attenuated.  
• No quantitative data on bone turnover markers in vivo.

────────────────────────────────────────────
6. Conclusion (weight-of-evidence)
────────────────────────────────────────────
Based on high systemic availability and a strong mechanistic rationale (sustained NMDA receptor antagonism), CPM-DHI given orally at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days is expected to produce:

• Mild, reversible skeletal muscle atrophy secondary to hypoactivity (adaptive).  
• Statistically significant, adverse but reversible reductions in trabecular bone mass and mineralisation (direct pharmacological effect plus disuse).  
• Changes are monitorable in the clinic (CK, physical performance, DXA).  
No frank myonecrosis, rhabdomyolysis or irreversible bone lesions are anticipated at the MTD.

These predictions should be verified by a guideline-compliant 28-day oral toxicity study that includes functional observational battery, limb grip strength, bone densitometry, and full musculoskeletal histopathology.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity.
